_short_description: Reproduces a high-restorativeness reading-room configuration consistent with Attention Restoration Theory (Kaplan & Kaplan, 1989): being-away from task demands, soft fascination through natural light and visual order, extent through coherent shelving, compatibility with quiet reading.
_published_finding: Restorative environments produce measurable cognitive recovery on directed-attention tasks. Hartig et al. (1991, 1996) showed natural-element exposure improved attention task performance vs urban controls; Berman, Jonides & Kaplan (2008) extended to working memory.
_caveats: ART components (being-away, soft fascination, extent, compatibility) interact non-linearly. This preset weights soft fascination and extent more heavily; the BN's L17 structured-factor (Sprint 3 contest #1) will read the four components separately.
The Berman et al. (2008) effect was for nature exposure; an interior library is a partial proxy. The preset should be validated against rated PRS scores from human raters.

Cross-cultural variance for restorativeness is medium per Sprint 2 panel. Asian temple-libraries and Western academic libraries elicit similar restorative responses in Yang et al. (2018) cross-cultural replication, but the colour palette skew may need cultural adaptation.

_citation.apa_primary: Kaplan, R., & Kaplan, S. (1989). The experience of nature: A psychological perspective. Cambridge University Press.
apa_supporting: Berman, M. G., Jonides, J., & Kaplan, S. (2008). The cognitive benefits of interacting with nature. Psychological Science, 19(12), 1207–1212.
Hartig, T., Korpela, K., Evans, G. W., & Gärling, T. (1997). A measure of restorative quality in environments. Scandinavian Housing and Planning Research, 14(4), 175–194.
